In other words, this result confirms that the square-root rule works for GI/GI/1 queue but not for the Hawkes/GI/1 queue especially when the increasing of average arrival rate is driven by the self-excitement effect, i.e. \(m \to 1\). We leave theoretic justification on this result for further study.

Since we show numerically that the square-root rule is not always satisfactory for Hawkes queues, one natural direction is to theoretically verify the numerical finding and identify the correct asymptotic staffing rule.
title: Determine the correct heavy-traffic staffing asymptotics for Hawkes single-server queues
background_latex:
Consider a single-server FIFO queue with constant service rate \(\mu>0\) and i.i.d. job sizes \(V\ge 0\) with \(\mathbb{E}[V]=1\). Customers arrive according to a stationary Hawkes process \(N(t)\) with baseline (immigrant) rate \(\lambda_0>0\) and excitation kernel \(h:\mathbb{R}_+\to\mathbb{R}_+\). The conditional intensity is
\[\lambda(t)=\lambda_0+\sum_{t_i<t} h(t-t_i).\]
Let \(m\equiv \int_0^\infty h(u)\,du\in(0,1)\) denote the branching ratio; then the stationary mean arrival rate is \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\). The workload process \(W(t)\) evolves as the Skorokhod reflection of
\[R(t)=\sum_{n=1}^{N(t)} V_n-\mu t,\]
so \(W(t)\ge 0\) and \(W(t)=W(0)+R(t)-\min_{0\le s\le t}\{0,\,W(0)+R(s)\}.\)
Assume stability in the sense \(\lambda\,\mathbb{E}[V]=\lambda<\mu\). For each \(\mu\), let \(W_\infty(\mu)\) denote the steady-state workload (a random variable with the stationary distribution of \(W(t)\)).

Consider the classic capacity-sizing/staffing objective
\[ f(\mu)=h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu), \]
where \(h_0>0\) is a holding-cost coefficient and \(c(\mu)\) is a staffing cost. In many renewal-arrival models (e.g., GI/GI/1 under appropriate regimes), heavy-traffic optimal staffing often satisfies a “square-root” scaling of the form \(\mu^*=\lambda+O(\sqrt{\lambda})\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (with \(\lambda\) the mean arrival rate). This paper reports numerical evidence that such square-root scaling can fail for Hawkes/GI/1 queues when the increase in \(\lambda\) is driven by strong self-excitation (e.g., \(m\uparrow 1\)).
problem_definition_latex:
In a heavy-traffic regime for Hawkes/GI/1 queues in which the mean arrival rate \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\) becomes large (for example, along a sequence with fixed \(\lambda_0\) and \(m\uparrow 1\)), identify and justify the correct asymptotic staffing rule for the optimizer \(\mu^*\) of the capacity-sizing problem
\[\mu^*\in\arg\min_{\mu\in B}\; \bigl(h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu)\bigr),\]
including the correct scaling of \(\mu^*-\lambda\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (or equivalently as \(m\uparrow 1\) in the above parameterization).

Consider a single-server FIFO queue with constant service rate \(\mu>0\) and i.i.d. job sizes \(V\ge 0\) with \(\mathbb{E}[V]=1\). Customers arrive according to a stationary Hawkes process \(N(t)\) with baseline (immigrant) rate \(\lambda_0>0\) and excitation kernel \(h:\mathbb{R}_+\to\mathbb{R}_+\). The conditional intensity is
\[\lambda(t)=\lambda_0+\sum_{t_i<t} h(t-t_i).\]
Let \(m\equiv \int_0^\infty h(u)\,du\in(0,1)\) denote the branching ratio; then the stationary mean arrival rate is \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\). The workload process \(W(t)\) evolves as the Skorokhod reflection of
\[R(t)=\sum_{n=1}^{N(t)} V_n-\mu t,\]
so \(W(t)\ge 0\) and \(W(t)=W(0)+R(t)-\min_{0\le s\le t}\{0,\,W(0)+R(s)\}.\)
Assume stability in the sense \(\lambda\,\mathbb{E}[V]=\lambda<\mu\). For each \(\mu\), let \(W_\infty(\mu)\) denote the steady-state workload (a random variable with the stationary distribution of \(W(t)\)).

Consider the classic capacity-sizing/staffing objective
\[ f(\mu)=h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu), \]
where \(h_0>0\) is a holding-cost coefficient and \(c(\mu)\) is a staffing cost. In many renewal-arrival models (e.g., GI/GI/1 under appropriate regimes), heavy-traffic optimal staffing often satisfies a “square-root” scaling of the form \(\mu^*=\lambda+O(\sqrt{\lambda})\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (with \(\lambda\) the mean arrival rate). This paper reports numerical evidence that such square-root scaling can fail for Hawkes/GI/1 queues when the increase in \(\lambda\) is driven by strong self-excitation (e.g., \(m\uparrow 1\)).

In a heavy-traffic regime for Hawkes/GI/1 queues in which the mean arrival rate \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\) becomes large (for example, along a sequence with fixed \(\lambda_0\) and \(m\uparrow 1\)), identify and justify the correct asymptotic staffing rule for the optimizer \(\mu^*\) of the capacity-sizing problem
\[\mu^*\in\arg\min_{\mu\in B}\; \bigl(h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu)\bigr),\]

location_in_paper: Section 8.3 (end of the section), page 30; reiterated in Conclusion (Section 9), page 31.
open_problem_quote:
In other words, this result confirms that the square-root rule works for GI/GI/1 queue but not for the Hawkes/GI/1 queue especially when the increasing of average arrival rate is driven by the self-excitement effect, i.e. \(m \to 1\). We leave theoretic justification on this result for further study.

Since we show numerically that the square-root rule is not always satisfactory for Hawkes queues, one natural direction is to theoretically verify the numerical finding and identify the correct asymptotic staffing rule.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The open question asks for an asymptotic characterization rather than the validity of a specific finite-dimensional inequality, so there is no crisp finite instance space where a single counterexample would resolve the problem. Simulation can suggest scaling behavior but cannot certify the correct asymptotic rule.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize candidate staffing scalings (e.g., \(\mu^*-\lambda\propto \lambda^\alpha\) with tunable \(\alpha\)) and use simulation-based estimation of \(\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]\) to fit/compare asymptotic regimes as a computable fitness signal. However, the core task is a theoretical proof/limit theorem, and simulations may be a noisy proxy for the true asymptotics.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The heavy-traffic staffing question for Hawkes/GI/1 queues in the near-critical self-excitation regime \(m\uparrow 1\) (so \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\to\infty\) with fixed \(\lambda_0\)) remains open in the forward-citation set provided. The most directly relevant progress is the development of a rigorous steady-state framework for Hawkes/GI/1: using the cluster (branching) representation of Hawkes processes, one can construct the stationary workload via a Loynes-type supremum representation \(W_\infty(\mu)=\sup_{s\ge 0} R^{\leftarrow}(0,s)\), prove polynomial and exponential moment bounds by domination with an auxiliary \(M/GI/1\) queue built from cluster departure times, and establish exponential ergodicity via a semi-synchronous coupling that explicitly tracks the Hawkes “memory” \(N_0(t)\). These results justify steady-state simulation/optimization and provide structural identities useful for staffing.

A key identity derived in this line of work is a gradient representation for the steady-state mean workload \(w(\mu)=\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]\): under stability and light-tail assumptions, \(w'(\mu)=-\mathbb{E}[X_\infty(\mu)]\), where \(X_\infty(\mu)\) is the stationary observed busy-period age. This connects staffing optimization \(h_0 w(\mu)+c(\mu)\) to the steady-state busy-period functional and enables stochastic-gradient methods. Numerically, in the regime \(m\uparrow 1\) with fixed \(\lambda_0\), the reported optimizers \(\mu_H^*\) exhibit \((\mu_H^* - \lambda)/\sqrt{\lambda}\) increasing with \(\lambda\), indicating that the classical square-root safety staffing \(\mu^* = \lambda + O(\sqrt{\lambda})\) can fail when the growth in \(\lambda\) is driven by self-excitation rather than by scaling up exogenous arrivals.

What is missing is a heavy-traffic limit theorem and associated steady-state approximation in the near-critical Hawkes regime that would yield the correct order of \(\mu^* - \lambda\) and a limiting optimization problem. One plausible direction is that near-critical Hawkes input induces long memory/slowly decaying correlations in the netput process, potentially leading to nonstandard (non-Markovian) reflected limits; in that case, tools for reflected diffusions with memory (e.g., fractional drift) could become relevant for characterizing stationary performance. Establishing such a limit and then optimizing the limiting objective would be the natural route to a rigorous staffing asymptotic.

sources.title: Time-Varying Single-Server Queues with State-Dependent Hawkes Arrivals
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves steady-state moment bounds and exponential ergodicity for Hawkes/GI/1 queues under light-tail assumptions, and derives a gradient representation for the staffing objective: if \(w(\mu)=\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]\), then \(w'(\mu)=-\mathbb{E}[X_\infty(\mu)]\), where \(X_\infty(\mu)\) is the steady-state observed busy-period age. It also provides numerical experiments showing that in a regime where \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\to\infty\) via \(m\uparrow 1\) (fixed \(\lambda_0\)), the ratio \((\mu_H^*-\lambda)/\sqrt{\lambda}\) increases with \(\lambda\), suggesting failure of square-root staffing for Hawkes/GI/1.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the correct heavy-traffic staffing asymptotics for Hawkes/GI/1 when \(\lambda\) grows due to strong self-excitation (e.g. \(m\uparrow 1\)). This paper does not derive an asymptotic characterization of \(\mu^*\) or the scaling of \(\mu^*-\lambda\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\). However, it contributes (i) rigorous steady-state control (finite exponential moments, exponential convergence) that can justify simulation/optimization and potentially underpin heavy-traffic limits, and (ii) explicit numerical evidence (Table 2) that \(\mu_H^*-\lambda\) grows faster than \(O(\sqrt{\lambda})\) in the \(m\uparrow 1\) regime, aligning with the open problem’s conjecture. The paper explicitly states that theoretical justification and identification of the correct asymptotic rule are left for future work.

sources.title: Stationary distribution and exponential ergodicity of a reflected diffusion with fractional drift
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only: the paper studies a one-dimensional reflected diffusion on \([0,\infty)\) with a fractional (memory/long-range dependent) drift term, proves exponential ergodicity, and gives an explicit characterization of the stationary distribution (described as a sub-… likely a subordinator-based representation).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for heavy-traffic staffing asymptotics for Hawkes/GI/1 queues as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (e.g., \(m\uparrow 1\)), which typically requires identifying the correct heavy-traffic diffusion (or other) limit for the workload and then optimizing \(h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu)\). A reflected diffusion with fractional drift could be relevant if the Hawkes-driven workload, under near-critical self-excitation, converges to a reflected process with non-Markovian/fractional drift; then this paper’s stationary distribution and ergodicity results could help approximate \(\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]\) and thus staffing. However, based only on the abstract, there is no explicit link to Hawkes queues, no staffing optimization, and no stated scaling for \(\mu^*-\lambda\).
